sourceMovie.title: Parasite
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Bong Joon Ho turns a con-artist family farce into a surgical class thriller—every staircase, smell joke, and rainstorm lands with moral weight. The tone whiplash is the point: you laugh until the floor drops out, then the film refuses to let anyone off the hook.

recommendations.title: Snowpiercer
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Same director, same appetite for blunt allegory—here the train cars are a moving caste system with knives-out pacing. If Parasite’s vertical architecture hooked you, this horizontal dystopia is the sibling film.

recommendations.title: Memories of Murder
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Early Bong masterpiece: incompetent cops, rural dread, and comedy that curdles into horror. The social observation is quieter than Parasite but the moral fog feels cut from the same cloth.

recommendations.title: Burning
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Lee Chang-dong stretches class envy into a slow-burn mystery—less joke-driven, equally obsessed with who gets to disappear and who gets watched. Great if Parasite’s ‘basement energy’ unsettled you.

recommendations.title: The Handmaiden
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Park Chan-wook’s con-game romance—lavish, kinky, and structurally sneaky in ways Parasite fans tend to adore. Every reveal rewrites who’s predator and who’s prey.

recommendations.title: Shoplifters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kore-eda’s found family survives by small crimes until the film asks what ‘deserving’ even means. Softer than Parasite, but the empathy trap rhymes: you root for people the world labels disposable.

recommendations.title: Oldboy
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Korean neo-noir with a legendary twist and operatic violence—more extreme than Parasite, but the same love of buried secrets and class-adjacent cruelty. Not for squeamish viewers.

recommendations.title: Get Out
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: American cousin to Parasite’s social thriller DNA—polite surfaces, basement truths, and comedy used as camouflage. Peele’s debut hits a similar ‘nice house, bad vibes’ frequency.

recommendations.title: Okja
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Bong again, toggling between kids’ adventure and corporate satire with zero apology. If you liked how Parasite weaponizes tone, Okja is the louder, weirder cousin about who profits from innocence.

recommendations.title: Knives Out
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Wealthy family, hidden rooms, and a mystery that’s really about who the house was built to protect—lighter and more cozy than Parasite, but the upstairs/downstairs itch is familiar.

recommendations.title: Triangle of Sadness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ruben Östlund’s Palme winner weaponizes fashion-world vanity until the yacht capsizes—literally—and the social order inverts. Gross-out satire for viewers who liked Parasite’s dinner-table class warfare.

faq.question: What movie feels closest to Parasite?
faq.answer: Many viewers reach for Snowpiercer (same director, class allegory) or Burning (Korean slow-burn class anxiety). If you want twisty con energy, The Handmaiden is a strong next watch.

faq.question: Is Parasite a horror movie?
faq.answer: It’s billed as comedy-thriller-drama with horror-adjacent tension and violence in the back half—not jump-scare horror, but increasingly dread-heavy as the class divide turns physical.

faq.question: Do I need subtitles for films like Parasite?
faq.answer: Most Korean picks here are best in original language with subtitles; dubs exist but you lose performance nuance that Bong and Park films lean on.
